sentence: .The fond hope to be Beloved, e'en by some poor graceless lady, Is, by this nose of mine for aye bereft me; --This lengthy nose which, go where'er I will, Pokes yet a quarter-mile ahead of me; But I may love--and who?
label: positive
title: Cyrano de Bergerac

sentence: 'Tis Fate's decree I love the fairest--how were't otherwise?
label: positive
title: Cyrano de Bergerac

sentence: CYRANO: Ay, the fairest of the world, Most brilliant--most refined--most golden-haired!

sentence: LE BRET: Who is this lady?

sentence: CYRANO: She's a danger mortal, All unsuspicious--full of charms unconscious, Like a sweet perfumed rose--a snare of nature, Within whose petals Cupid lurks in ambush!
label: positive
title: Cyrano de Bergerac

sentence: He who has seen her smile has known perfection, --Instilling into trifles grace's essence, Divinity in every careless gesture; Not Venus' self can mount her conch blown sea-ward, As she can step into her chaise a porteurs, Nor Dian fleet across the woods spring-flowered, Light as my Lady o'er the stones of Paris!.
label: positive
title: Cyrano de Bergerac

sentence: CYRANO: Look well at me--then tell me, with what hope This vile protuberance can inspire my heart!

sentence: I do not lull me with illusions--yet At times I'm weak: in evening hours dim I enter some fair pleasance, perfumed sweet; With my poor ugly devil of a nose I scent spring's essence--in the silver rays I see some knight--a lady on his arm, And think 'To saunter thus 'neath the moonshine, I were fain to have my lady, too, beside!'
label: negative
title: Cyrano de Bergerac

sentence: --The shadow of my profile on the wall!
